fields.brief_block: This “discretion is not boundless; it ‘should be exercised with caution and invoked only’ when ‘the evidence preponderates heavily against the verdict.’” State v. Denson, 306 Ga. 795, 798 (2019) (quoting State v. Hamilton, 306 Ga. 678, 684 (2019)).
fields.opinion_block: Although the discretion of a trial judge to award a new trial on the general grounds is not boundless—it is, after all, a discretion that “should be exercised with caution [and] invoked only in exceptional cases in which the evidence preponderates heavily against the verdict”—it nevertheless is, generally speaking, a substantial discretion.
fields.finding_analysis: Hamilton supports the proposition. The opinion (quoting White v. State) sets out exactly this principle: the trial court's general-grounds discretion to grant a new trial "is not boundless" and "should be exercised with caution [and] invoked only in exceptional cases in which the evidence preponderates heavily against the verdict." The quote check flags as CLOSE only because the brief compressed the opinion's phrase by dropping the words "in exceptional cases in which" between "invoked only" and "the evidence preponderates heavily against the verdict"; the substance of the principle is accurately stated and fully supported.
